principles.description: When a structural boundary needs to move, the natural design is not a hard cut (rename, migrate, force). It is a growth event: create the new layer, notify the old layer that the living wood has moved, and let everyone lay down new fibers in the new location. The old layer becomes heartwood — historical, immutable, structurally present but no longer the active growth surface. The new layer is the living sapwood — where current work accumulates, where new fibers form. This is the tree's own mechanism for structural change: it does not remove old rings to make new ones. It grows outward. The old rings become the core. The event horizon forms at the moment the first new fiber is laid in the new layer — not at the moment the old layer is declared finished. APPLIED: to move from EXCHANGE to TRUNK, you do not rename and migrate. You create ops-hub/TRUNK/, leave a notice in EXCHANGE that the living wood has moved to TRUNK, update canonical path references to point to TRUNK, and let everyone file new work in TRUNK from that point forward. EXCHANGE becomes the prior ring — growth rings from the founding cycle. TRUNK is the new cambium surface. Nothing is lost. Nothing is migrated. The tree grew.
principles.source: Troy, 2026-03-09 — 'if we followed the natural design, we would create a new folder, let everyone in the exchange folder that the trunk has moved to /trunk - from there everyone lays down wood in the new folder'

principles.name: The Trunk Split — Maximum Big Bang
principles.description: Cutting your trunk in half — splitting the system into two divergent lines — is the largest possible big bang. It is not a failure or a break. It is the system saying: we have enough mass in two directions that they can no longer share the same trunk. The split is earned, not forced. Each new trunk carries the full compression history of the original. This is how the fractal scales beyond one practitioner or one product line.
principles.source: troy-spark

principles.name: The Leaf Receives a Packet, Not the Trunk
principles.description: A leaf does not access the full trunk. The trunk distills everything down to the very tip — a packet containing only what the expression needs. The leaf renders the packet. The trunk owns the content update workflow. This is correct design: leaves need light, not roots. Architecturally, this means web pages, marketing surfaces, and product expressions read from static content files produced by the trunk — never from the trunk directly. The communication channel between leaf and trunk is a static artifact, versioned and updated intentionally.
principles.source: troy-spark

principles.name: Make Every Practitioner Switch Explicit
principles.description: Any time a practitioner or context change is signaled — 'pass to Fiona', 'Stewie online', 'let David know', 'Stan should handle this' — stop and ask explicitly: 'Is a practitioner switch needed? I'm currently [practitioner]. Should I stay on this or route it?' Do not assume. Do not silently continue. Make the switch visible and get a confirm. This keeps work routed correctly and prevents the wrong practitioner from executing the wrong domain's work.
principles.source: troy-spark

principles.name: Skills Live in the Trunk
principles.description: Skills are not practitioner-specific artifacts — they are trunk-level assets. Skills stored in the TRUNK are available to all practitioners and sessions. The trunk distills capability the same way it distills content. A skill is a packet: compressed, versioned, deployable. Filing a skill to the TRUNK makes it findable by any session operating on this system.
principles.source: troy-spark

principles.name: Practitioners Are Dual-Edge Nodes
principles.description: Practitioners Are Dual-Edge Nodes — every practitioner operates at two edges simultaneously in the gap between trees. Decomposition edge: pick up leaves from any product tree, break them down, return soil (condensed knowledge that feeds all trees). Growth edge: read the tip of the branch — the most compressed, most recent expression point — and grow the next leaf from it. You do not live in any product. You move through the spaces between them.
principles.source: troy-spark

principles.name: Closing the Loop Creates New Soil
principles.description: Practitioners are ephemeral — context-bound units that pick up small resources from the external world, carry them into the trunk, and contribute to expressions that fall outward. When those expressions complete — when the loop closes — they return as visible artifacts in space. That act of closing is what generates new soil. Soil is not the beginning; it is the product of completed cycles. The system renews itself not by accumulation but by expression and return.
principles.source: troy-spark

principles.name: Practitioners Save Their Place on Switch
principles.description: Any time a practitioner hands off to another practitioner, they write a switch note to the TRUNK before leaving. The note records: current state, what was just completed, what is in progress, what is blocked, what the next practitioner needs to know to pick up cleanly. This is how context survives the switch. A practitioner who switches without writing to the trunk leaves the next practitioner blind and forces redundant re-orientation work. The switch note is the fiber between sessions.
principles.source: troy-directive

principles.name: Collaboration Compression Routing
principles.description: Collaboration output compresses into both the discipline NDD soil and the core TRUNK simultaneously. Individual practitioner output routes to either the TRUNK or the practitioner own NDD based on scope — cross-practitioner insight goes to the discipline NDD and TRUNK; individual discovery goes to the practitioner NDD first. No insight that emerges from collaboration is lost — it is compressed twice, at discipline level and at trunk level.
principles.source: troy-directive

principles.name: NDD-First Writing Protocol
principles.description: All practitioners write to their NDD before writing to the TRUNK. The NDD receives raw knowledge — uncompressed, freshly observed. The TRUNK receives the compressed output. Write order is non-negotiable: soil deposit first, then compress, then route to TRUNK. This prevents the TRUNK from becoming a write-first-think-later repository. The NDD is the ground. The TRUNK is what grows from it.
principles.source: troy-directive

principles.name: Trunk-as-Code-Substrate
principles.description: All operational code, scripts, and task definitions that run the internal system live in the TRUNK. Not in separate scripts/ directories, not scattered across project repositories. The TRUNK is the single substrate for both knowledge work and the code that processes it. Code files use the prefix code_ and are versioned independently. No operational code lives outside the TRUNK unless it is a project-specific implementation rather than system-level tooling.
principles.source: troy-directive

principles.name: Output Versioning with Trunk Core
principles.description: All generated outputs — HTML files, JSON exports, reports, indexes — carry a trunk version stamp at time of generation. Version format: v{N}_{YYYYMMDD}_{HHMM}. The trunk version is tracked in ops-hub/TRUNK/TRUNK_VERSION.json. The version increments on every significant write to the TRUNK. An output without a trunk version stamp is not a canonical output. Versioning is automatic — no practitioner generates an output file without it.
principles.source: troy-directive
